q: What are the four workspace roles in Microsoft Fabric and what distinguishes each one from the others?
a: Microsoft Fabric workspaces use four roles arranged in ascending permission order: Viewer, Contributor, Member, and Admin. Viewers can read and interact with content but cannot create or modify items. Contributors can create, edit, and delete items inside the workspace, but they cannot reshare content or publish to an app audience. Members add the ability to reshare content, publish and update workspace apps, and allow others to reshare items. Admins have full control including adding or removing users of any role, deleting the workspace, and managing all settings. Roles are assigned to individual users, security groups, Microsoft 365 groups, or distribution lists. As of August 2025, each workspace is limited to a maximum of 1,000 principals across all roles to ensure service reliability.
fj: The Contributor role allows users to reshare items with external users.
fs: Resharing requires at minimum the Member role. Contributors can create and edit content inside the workspace, but the ability to grant others access or reshare is reserved for Members and Admins.

q: A team wants to give a data analyst read-only access to all items in a Fabric workspace, but should not be able to edit anything. A second analyst needs to build new reports but must not be able to invite additional users. Which roles should each receive?
a: The read-only analyst should receive the Viewer role, which grants access to view all items and interact with reports but prevents any create, edit, or delete operations. The report-building analyst should receive the Contributor role, which permits creating and editing semantic models, reports, notebooks, and other items without the ability to manage workspace membership or reshare content to others. Assigning Member to the second analyst would be over-privileged because Member grants the ability to reshare content and publish apps. Following the principle of least privilege is important in Fabric because workspace roles are broad and apply to every item in the workspace; for more granular per-item access you must combine workspace roles with item-level permissions such as Build permission on a semantic model.
fj: Assign Member to the report-building analyst because Contributor cannot publish reports.
fs: Contributors can publish reports to the workspace. The Member role is not needed for report authoring—it is only needed when a person must manage app audiences or reshare workspace items to external users.

q: How do you apply a sensitivity label to a semantic model in Microsoft Fabric, and what downstream effect does label inheritance have?
a: Sensitivity labels are defined in Microsoft Purview and published through label policies to users and groups. In Fabric, you apply a label by opening the item's settings and selecting a label from the dropdown, or programmatically via the Fabric REST API or the Purview public API (generally available as of March 2026). You need at minimum Edit permissions on the item to apply or change a label. Downstream inheritance means that when a report or semantic model is built on top of a labeled source, the higher-sensitivity label propagates automatically to the downstream artifact—ensuring a report built on a Confidential semantic model cannot be accidentally published without the same classification. When content is exported (to Excel, PDF, or PowerPoint), the sensitivity label and any associated encryption travel with the file, enforcing protection outside of Fabric.
fj: Sensitivity labels in Fabric only add a visual watermark and do not affect data access or export behavior.
fs: Sensitivity labels can be linked to Microsoft Purview Information Protection policies that apply encryption and restrict who can open or edit an exported file. They are not purely cosmetic—labels can block download, enforce encryption on exports, and trigger audit events in the Purview compliance portal.

code.label: Apply a sensitivity label via Fabric REST API
lines: # Get an Entra access token scoped to Power BI
# Item to label: semantic model in a workspace

q: How do you configure Git integration (version control) for a Microsoft Fabric workspace, and which Git providers are supported?
a: Git integration is configured at the workspace level from Workspace settings under the Source Control section. Fabric supports Azure DevOps and GitHub as Git providers. For Azure DevOps, authentication uses the Microsoft Entra identity you are already signed into Fabric with—no additional token is needed. For GitHub, you generate a Personal Access Token (PAT) with the required repository scopes and enter it in the connection dialog. After connecting, you specify the organization, project, repository, and target branch. Fabric serializes workspace items (reports, semantic models, notebooks, lakehouses, etc.) as human-readable files in the repository—TMDL for semantic models and JSON for report definitions. Each developer can connect their own private branch to their own workspace, commit changes there, and open a pull request back to the shared branch. The tenant admin must enable Git integration in Admin portal tenant settings before workspace admins can configure it.
fj: Git integration in Fabric works at the item level—you connect a single report or semantic model to a repository, not the entire workspace.
fs: Fabric's Git integration is workspace-scoped. The entire workspace is connected to a single branch, and all eligible items in that workspace are tracked together. You cannot selectively connect only some items; instead, use branch strategies and separate workspaces per developer to isolate changes.

q: What is a Power BI Desktop project (.pbip) file and how does its folder structure benefit a team using version control?
a: A Power BI Desktop project (.pbip) is a save format that decomposes a Power BI report into a folder tree of plain-text files instead of a single binary .pbix file. The folder contains a .pbip descriptor file that points Power BI Desktop to the content, a Report subfolder with JSON files in Power BI Enhanced Report Format (PBIR) describing every page, visual, bookmark, and filter, and a SemanticModel subfolder containing Tabular Model Definition Language (TMDL) files—one per table, measure, relationship, and role. Because every artifact is text, developers can diff changes between commits, perform line-level code reviews on DAX measures or M queries, resolve merge conflicts in a standard Git workflow, and automate CI/CD pipelines that validate or deploy the model. PBIR splits each report page into its own file, making merge conflicts on separate pages extremely rare. This replaces the old .pbix binary format that was unreadable by diff tools.
fj: A .pbip file is simply a renamed .pbix file that stores data in a compressed ZIP archive.
fs: A .pbip is a project descriptor pointing to a folder of text files; no data is stored in it at all. A .pbix is a binary ZIP that bundles the data cache, report, and model together, making it unsuitable for version control. The key distinction is that .pbip enables Git-friendly, line-level diffing.

q: Describe how to create and configure a deployment pipeline in Microsoft Fabric, including how deployment rules work.
a: A deployment pipeline is created from the Fabric workspace list by selecting 'Create deployment pipeline'. You define between 2 and 10 stages at creation time—this number is permanent and cannot be changed later. Default stage names are Development, Test, and Production, but you can rename them. Each stage is associated with one Fabric workspace; you assign existing workspaces or create new ones per stage. To deploy, you select items in the source stage and click Deploy to the next stage—Fabric copies item definitions without moving underlying OneLake data. Deployment rules let you override specific settings per target stage so the same item behaves differently in each environment. The three rule types are: data source rules (redirect a data source URL to the environment-specific endpoint), parameter rules (override Power Query parameters), and default lakehouse rules (swap which lakehouse a notebook or pipeline targets). Rules are created in the target stage and only take effect once you deploy from source to target after rule creation.
fj: Deployment pipeline rules are applied in the source stage before deployment begins.
fs: Rules are defined in and applied to the target stage, not the source. When you deploy from Development to Test, the rules configured in Test override the source item's settings. Creating a rule in the source stage has no effect on the deployment outcome.

q: How do you use the XMLA endpoint to deploy a semantic model to a Fabric workspace, and which tools support this workflow?
a: The XMLA endpoint exposes a workspace as an Analysis Services server endpoint using the tabular protocol. Every Fabric F-SKU and Premium P-SKU capacity has XMLA read/write enabled by default as of June 2025. To find the endpoint URL, open Workspace settings > Premium and copy the XMLA endpoint string (format: powerbi://api.powerbi.com/v1.0/myorg/<WorkspaceName>). You can then connect with: Tabular Editor (TE2 open-source or TE3), SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS), ALM Toolkit, or PowerShell via the Analysis Services .NET client library. Tabular Editor lets you open or create a model definition, modify tables, measures, relationships, and then Save to Fabric, which writes metadata via XMLA write without a data refresh. ALM Toolkit performs a schema diff between a source model and the target workspace model, then deploys only the changed metadata—ideal for CI/CD pipelines that must preserve incremental refresh partitions. XMLA-deployed models are fully editable in the Fabric web UI after deployment.
fj: XMLA is read-only; you can query a model through it but cannot deploy or modify a semantic model's structure.
fs: XMLA read/write is supported on all Fabric F-SKUs and Premium P-SKUs. In read/write mode you can create new models, modify table structures, add measures, and script deployments—not just query. Read-only XMLA was the historical limitation for Power BI Pro workspaces, which no longer applies for Fabric capacities.

code.label: Deploy semantic model via XMLA using SSAS .NET (PowerShell)

q: What is the difference between a .pbit template file, a .pbids data source file, and a shared semantic model in Microsoft Fabric, and when would you use each?
a: A .pbit (Power BI Template) file contains the report layout, visuals, data model schema, relationships, and measures—but no actual data. When a user opens it, Power BI Desktop prompts for parameter values and refreshes to load data from the source. Use .pbit to distribute a standard report structure across teams so everyone starts from the same model and visual design without sharing cached data. A .pbids (Power BI Data Source) file contains only the connection string for one or more data sources—no model, no visuals. Opening it launches Power BI Desktop and pre-fills the data source connection, guiding users to connect to the correct server or lakehouse endpoint. Use .pbids to standardize data source connections across a team. A shared semantic model (live connection or DirectQuery to a published Fabric semantic model) is the most powerful reuse pattern: multiple reports point to one centrally governed model in the Fabric service; any DAX measures or table changes in the model are reflected immediately across all connected reports. Use shared models when you want a single source of truth for business metrics governed by a central team.
fj: A .pbit file contains the full data refresh so users can open it without connecting to any source.
fs: A .pbit strips the data cache before saving. It saves the model schema and report visuals, but users must supply connection credentials and trigger a refresh when they open the file. The absence of cached data keeps the file small and avoids distributing sensitive data in the template.

q: How do you discover data assets using the OneLake catalog in Microsoft Fabric, and how does the Real-Time hub complement it for streaming sources?
a: The OneLake catalog is the central governance and discovery UI in Microsoft Fabric accessible from the left nav. It provides a unified view of all Fabric items across workspaces you have access to—lakehouses, warehouses, semantic models, eventhouses, reports, notebooks, and more—along with their sensitivity labels, endorsement status (Certified or Promoted), owners, and lineage. You can filter by item type, workspace, domain, or sensitivity label to find relevant assets. The Explore tab lets you browse items and preview schemas without opening each workspace individually. The Real-Time hub complements the catalog for streaming and event-driven data: it surfaces Fabric event streams, KQL databases in Eventhouses, external streaming sources (Azure Event Hubs, Kafka, IoT Hub), and OneLake events (file-created, file-deleted triggers). Together, the catalog handles discovery of batch/analytical assets while the Real-Time hub surfaces live streams and enables subscribing to events for real-time processing pipelines.
fj: The OneLake catalog is only available to Fabric administrators and is not meant for data analysts to find assets.
fs: The OneLake catalog is designed for all Fabric users including data engineers, analysts, and decision-makers. Access is governed by workspace roles and item permissions—users see only items they are allowed to access. Admins have additional governance views, but the catalog is a self-service discovery tool for the entire organization.

q: What is OneLake integration for an Eventhouse in Microsoft Fabric and how does enabling it affect data availability?
a: OneLake integration for an Eventhouse (KQL database) automatically mirrors the data stored in KQL tables to Delta Parquet format in OneLake. When you enable it on a KQL database, Fabric continuously exports the data as Delta tables into a managed OneLake path, making the same real-time data accessible to Fabric workloads that read Delta—lakehouses, notebooks, Spark, semantic models, and SQL analytics endpoints—without any ETL pipelines or manual export steps. The KQL database remains the primary write and query surface for real-time analytics, while the OneLake Delta path provides a read path for batch and BI workloads. For semantic models, enabling OneLake integration allows imported tables to be automatically written to Delta tables in OneLake, so downstream workloads can read the semantic model's cached data without querying the model's XMLA endpoint. This creates a governed, Delta-format copy of the semantic model's data that participates in OneLake lineage.
fj: OneLake integration for Eventhouse copies raw message bytes from the event stream into ADLS Gen2 in JSON format.
fs: OneLake integration for Eventhouse converts KQL table data into Delta Parquet format—not raw JSON event bytes. Delta format allows the data to be read by Spark, SQL analytics endpoints, and semantic models using standard Fabric compute, which raw JSON blobs do not support natively.

q: How do you create views and stored procedures in a Fabric Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, and what are their limitations?
a: Every Fabric lakehouse automatically provisions a SQL analytics endpoint—a read-only T-SQL surface over its Delta tables. You can enrich this endpoint by writing CREATE VIEW and CREATE PROCEDURE statements using the SQL query editor in the Fabric UI or any SSMS/Azure Data Studio connection to the endpoint. Views encapsulate reusable JOIN and transformation logic so downstream reports and notebooks can query a simplified surface without knowing the underlying Delta table structure. Stored procedures allow multi-step T-SQL logic including variables, conditional branches, and loops to be stored and reused. A key limitation is that the SQL analytics endpoint is read-only with respect to the underlying lakehouse Delta files—you cannot run INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on the auto-generated tables; those operations must go through Spark or the Warehouse. User-created objects (views, procedures, functions) are stored in the endpoint's SQL metadata layer and survive lakehouse schema changes. Stored procedures at the SQL analytics endpoint support SELECT-based logic but cannot perform DML against Delta tables.
fj: You cannot create stored procedures in a lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint—it only supports ad-hoc queries.
fs: Fabric's SQL analytics endpoint supports CREATE PROCEDURE, CREATE VIEW, and CREATE FUNCTION using the T-SQL editor in the Fabric UI or SSMS. These objects persist in the endpoint's SQL metadata store and are separate from the auto-synchronized Delta table catalog. The endpoint runs on the same engine as Fabric Data Warehouse, providing full T-SQL object support.

code.label: Create a view and stored procedure in Fabric SQL analytics endpoint
lines: -- Create a view summarizing daily sales
JOIN dbo.dim_Date d ON f.DateKey = d.DateKey
JOIN dbo.dim_Product p ON f.ProductKey = p.ProductKey
-- Create a stored procedure to return top-N products
SELECT TOP (@TopN) ProductName, SUM(TotalSales) AS Revenue

q: What are the design principles for implementing a star schema in a Fabric Lakehouse or Warehouse, and how do you load it correctly?
a: A star schema in Fabric consists of a central fact table containing measurable events (sales, clicks, orders) connected through foreign keys to denormalized dimension tables (date, customer, product, geography). In a Fabric Lakehouse you create Delta tables using Spark; in a Fabric Warehouse you use T-SQL DDL. Dimension tables should be loaded before fact tables on each pipeline run to prevent orphaned foreign key references. Use surrogate integer keys (auto-increment or IDENTITY in the Warehouse) rather than natural business keys as join keys to support slowly changing dimensions (SCD). Prefix fact tables with fact_ and dimension tables with dim_ for clarity. For SCD Type 2 dimensions, add StartDate, EndDate, and IsCurrent columns to track historical attribute changes. Partition fact tables by date on large datasets to improve query pruning. Use schema containers (lakehouse schemas) to logically separate gold-layer dimensional objects from staging tables. Keep dimension tables narrow and avoid inner joins from facts to multiple facts, which breaks the star topology.
fj: In a star schema you should normalize dimension tables into a snowflake to reduce storage size in Fabric.
fs: Snowflake schemas add joins and complexity that degrade query performance in Fabric's VertiPaq-based semantic models. The star schema's denormalized dimensions trade a small amount of storage redundancy for significantly faster DAX aggregations and simpler report authoring. Fabric's columnar storage makes the redundancy cost negligible.

code.label: Star schema DDL in Fabric Data Warehouse
lines: DateKey INT NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
Year INT, Quarter INT, Month INT, MonthName VARCHAR(20)
StartDate DATE, EndDate DATE, IsCurrent BIT
DateKey INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dim_Date(DateKey),
CustomerKey INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dim_Customer(CustomerKey),

q: How do you convert column data types in a Fabric Lakehouse using PySpark, and what are common pitfalls to watch for?
a: In Fabric Lakehouse you transform data with PySpark in a notebook. Use the cast() method or the col().cast() pattern to convert column types. For example, converting a string date column to DateType uses col('order_date').cast('date'), and an integer amount stored as string uses col('amount').cast('double'). After casting, write back with df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('schema.table_name'). Common pitfalls include: silent nullification—when a cast fails (e.g., casting 'N/A' to integer), Spark returns null without raising an error by default, so always run a null count before and after to detect data loss. Schema evolution in Delta may require using mergeSchema or overwriteSchema options when the cast changes a column's type in an existing Delta table. Implicit widening casts (int to long) are safe, but narrowing casts (double to int) truncate decimal values. Always test casts on a sample with .show() before writing.
fj: Using df.withColumn('col', df['col'].cast('int')) automatically raises an exception if any value cannot be cast.
fs: PySpark casts fail silently—incompatible values become null rather than raising exceptions. To detect bad casts you must compare null counts before and after, or use a trycast approach by checking for nulls where the source was not null. Explicit error handling requires custom UDFs or null-check logic.

code.label: Type conversion with null-safety check in PySpark
lines: # Cast string columns to proper types
# Detect silent cast failures (nulls introduced by bad values)
print(f'New nulls introduced by cast: {null_after - null_before}')

q: What techniques do you use in PySpark to identify and resolve duplicate rows, missing values, and null fields in a Fabric Lakehouse?
a: For duplicates, use df.duplicated() equivalents: df.groupBy(key_cols).count().filter('count > 1') to find duplicate keys, then df.dropDuplicates(subset=key_cols) to keep one copy, or window functions with ROW_NUMBER() partitioned by key ordered by timestamp to keep the latest row. For missing values, df.filter(col('field').isNull() | col('field').isNaN()).count() quantifies the problem. Resolution options include df.dropna(subset=['critical_col']) to drop rows, df.fillna({'revenue': 0, 'region': 'Unknown'}) to impute with constants, or window-based forward-fill using last(col, ignorenulls=True) over a time-ordered window. For string fields containing empty strings masking as 'present', replace with when(col('field') == '', None) then treat as null. Always profile data quality before writing to Silver: count rows, count nulls per column, check distinct values on dimension keys to catch unexpected null foreign keys that would silently exclude fact rows from star-schema joins.
fj: df.dropna() without any arguments is a safe default for cleaning all nulls before loading to Silver.
fs: df.dropna() with no arguments drops any row that has a null in ANY column, which can silently discard most of your dataset if nullable optional fields are common. Always specify subset=['critical_col'] to target only columns where nulls are genuinely invalid, or use imputation strategies for non-critical fields.

code.label: Dedup and null handling in PySpark
lines: from pyspark.sql.functions import col, row_number, when
# --- Deduplication: keep latest row per event_id ---
# Replace empty strings with null first
# Impute nulls: 0 for numeric, 'Unknown' for string
# Drop rows where primary key is null (always invalid)

q: How do you implement a star schema for a Power BI semantic model in Microsoft Fabric, and why does the model topology matter for DAX?
a: In the Power BI semantic model (formerly 'dataset'), a star schema is built by importing or connecting to fact and dimension tables, then defining relationships in Model view with single-direction filters flowing from dimensions to facts. Each relationship should be many-to-one (fact to dimension) using integer surrogate keys. Avoid many-to-many relationships where possible—they require bidirectional filters that can produce incorrect results and performance degradation. Role-playing dimensions (e.g., a single dim_Date used for OrderDate, ShipDate, and DueDate) are handled by creating inactive relationships and activating them in DAX with USERELATIONSHIP(). The star topology matters for DAX because the filter context propagates along relationship directions—a filter on dim_Customer automatically filters fact_Sales because the relationship points in that direction. Snowflake joins (dim_Customer → dim_Region → dim_Country) create multi-hop filter propagation that DAX handles less efficiently than a single flat dimension, often causing slower measures. Keeping dimensions flat and wide is the recommended pattern for Power BI semantic models.
fj: Bidirectional cross-filtering between fact and dimension tables is recommended in Power BI to enable flexible slicing from either side.
fs: Bidirectional filtering is generally discouraged in star schemas because it can cause ambiguous filter paths, unexpected many-to-many cardinality effects, and slower query performance. The recommended pattern is single-direction filtering from dimension to fact. Use DAX functions like CROSSFILTER() only when a specific measure requires a non-default filter direction.

q: When should you enable the large semantic model storage format in Fabric, and what are the irreversible consequences of doing so?
a: Large semantic model storage format allows a semantic model to grow beyond its default per-model size limit and can be enabled on any Fabric F-SKU, Premium P-SKU, Embedded A-SKU, or Pro workspace assigned to Reserved Capacity. Use cases that require it include: models that exceed 10 GB after import refresh, high-cardinality models with hundreds of millions of rows, or models that leverage XMLA write operations heavily (large format improves XMLA write throughput). To enable it, a capacity admin first turns on the setting at the workspace level in Admin portal, then the workspace admin changes the semantic model's storage format in its settings. The critical operational consequence is that once large format is enabled on a semantic model, it cannot be reverted to small format. This means the model becomes permanently dependent on a Premium or Fabric capacity—it will stop working if the workspace is moved to a Pro or shared capacity that does not support large format. Capacity admins should verify the target workspace will remain on an eligible SKU before enabling.
fj: Large semantic model storage format is enabled per-capacity and applies automatically to all models in every workspace on that capacity.
fs: Large format is controlled at two levels: the capacity admin enables the option at the workspace level, and then it must be explicitly turned on for each individual semantic model. It is not an automatic blanket setting. This means models in the same workspace can have different storage formats, and enabling it on one model does not affect others.

q: How do you use DAX Studio and Best Practice Analyzer together to improve DAX performance in a Fabric semantic model?
a: The recommended workflow is: (1) In Power BI Desktop or the Fabric web, use the built-in Performance Analyzer pane to identify which report visuals are slowest and capture their DAX queries. (2) Paste those queries into DAX Studio, click 'Clear cache + run' to get cold timings that reflect real-world performance, then check the Server Timings tab to see the breakdown between Formula Engine (FE) and Storage Engine (SE) time. High FE time indicates complex DAX logic that should be simplified; high SE time indicates missing VertiPaq-friendly aggregations or high-cardinality columns. Use DAX Studio's query plan view (physical and logical) to spot CALLBACKDATAID callbacks, which are the most expensive SE operations. (3) Run Best Practice Analyzer (BPA) inside Tabular Editor against the same model to scan for rule violations: avoid bidirectional relationships, avoid using VALUE() in measures, use INT() for date keys, avoid IFERROR() wrapping CALCULATE(), ensure all relationships use integer keys, and mark date tables with MARK AS DATE TABLE. BPA rules are customizable and can be embedded in a CI pipeline to block rule violations before deployment.
fj: High Storage Engine time always indicates a data model problem that requires partitioning or aggregations.
fs: High SE time can result from high-cardinality columns, missing aggregation tables, or inefficient filter contexts—but it can also simply mean the query is scanning a large dataset correctly. The first step is to check if the SE query count is unusually high (many small SE requests from CROSSJOIN or SUMX iterations), which indicates a DAX rewrite is needed. High SE time on a single large scan may be acceptable and addressable with aggregation tables or incremental refresh.

code.label: DAX rewrite: replace SUMX row-by-row with a single CALCULATE+SUM
lines: -- Slow: SUMX iterates every row of fact_Sales
-- Faster: pre-multiply in the model as a calculated column,
-- or use SUM on a pre-computed LineAmount column
-- Or use CALCULATE to push filter into SE

q: What is Direct Lake mode in Microsoft Fabric semantic models, and how do you configure default fallback and refresh (framing) behavior?
a: Direct Lake is a semantic model storage mode unique to Microsoft Fabric that reads Delta Parquet files directly from OneLake into the VertiPaq in-memory engine without a full data import or a live DirectQuery roundtrip per DAX query. It combines Import's in-memory speed with the freshness of DirectQuery. Framing is Direct Lake's equivalent of a refresh—instead of copying data, the engine reads the Delta log to identify which Parquet files changed since the last frame, evicts only those column segments from memory, and remaps references; this typically completes in seconds. You configure fallback behavior only on Direct Lake on SQL endpoints: set the DirectLakeBehavior property in model settings to 'DirectLakeOnly' (errors if fallback is needed), 'DirectQueryOnly' (always use DirectQuery), or the default which allows automatic fallback to DirectQuery when the query exceeds capacity guardrails or touches unsupported features like SQL views. Direct Lake on OneLake does not support DirectQuery fallback at all—if a guardrail is breached, the query returns an error. Schedule framing by setting up an automatic refresh schedule; incremental framing (2025) reloads only changed row groups, making the operation much faster for append-only fact tables.
fj: Direct Lake mode performs a full data copy from OneLake into the semantic model's in-memory cache during every refresh, similar to Import mode.
fs: Direct Lake reads Delta Parquet files directly without copying data out of OneLake into a separate cache. Framing only updates column segment metadata and evicts changed segments—it does not move data. This is what makes Direct Lake faster to 'refresh' than Import mode and why storage is not duplicated.

q: When should you choose Direct Lake on OneLake over Direct Lake on SQL endpoints in a Fabric semantic model, and what are the key trade-offs?
a: Direct Lake on OneLake connects the semantic model directly to Delta tables in OneLake via OneLake APIs, while Direct Lake on SQL endpoints connects through the SQL analytics endpoint of a lakehouse or warehouse. Choose Direct Lake on OneLake when: your model needs tables from multiple Fabric items (e.g., fact from one lakehouse, dimensions from another), you want the highest possible performance without any SQL layer overhead, or you are authoring the model primarily in Power BI Desktop. Choose Direct Lake on SQL endpoints when: your security model uses SQL row-level security (RLS) defined in the SQL analytics endpoint, you need the model to fall back gracefully to DirectQuery when guardrails are exceeded rather than returning an error, or users create the model from the Fabric web UI without Power BI Desktop. The critical trade-offs are: (1) Multi-source support—OneLake supports multiple Fabric items; SQL supports only one. (2) Fallback—SQL endpoints fall back to DirectQuery on guardrail breaches; OneLake returns an error instead. (3) Security integration—SQL endpoints use the lakehouse's SQL-level granular access control; OneLake uses OneLake API permissions. (4) Authoring—OneLake models are created in Power BI Desktop; SQL models can be created in the Fabric web UI.
fj: Direct Lake on SQL endpoints is always faster than Direct Lake on OneLake because it uses a SQL query optimizer.
fs: Direct Lake on OneLake is generally faster because it reads Delta Parquet files directly without routing through a SQL engine layer. The SQL endpoint adds an intermediate processing step that can reduce throughput. The SQL endpoint variant's advantages are its fallback safety net, SQL-level security, and web-only creation—not query speed.
